([/ˈsloʊviːn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Slovene2.ogg "File:En-us-Slovene2.ogg") [*SLOH-veen*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") or [/sloʊˈviːn, slə-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*sloh-VEEN, slə-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")[[8]](#cite_note-8)) or **Slovenian** ([/sloʊˈviːniən, slə-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Slovenian.ogg "File:En-us-Slovenian.ogg") [*sloh-VEE-nee-ən, slə-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) *slovenščina* [[slɔˈʋèːnʃtʃina]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Slovene "Help:IPA/Slovene")) is a [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavic_languages "South Slavic languages") language of the [Balto-Slavic](/wiki/Balto-Slavic_languages "Balto-Slavic languages") branch of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"). Most of its 2.5 million speakers are the inhabitants of [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), the majority of them ethnic [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes"). As Slovenia is part of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), Slovene is also one of its 24 [official and working languages](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union"). Its grammar is highly [fusional](/wiki/Fusional_languages "Fusional languages"), and it has a [dual grammatical number](/wiki/Dual_(grammatical_number) "Dual (grammatical number)"), an archaic feature shared with some other Indo-European languages. Two accentual norms (one characterized by [pitch accent](/wiki/Pitch-accent_language "Pitch-accent language")) are used. Its flexible [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order") is often adjusted for emphasis or stylistic reasons, although basically it is an [SVO](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object_word_order "Subject–verb–object word order") language. It has a [T–V distinction](/wiki/T%E2%80%93V_distinction "T–V distinction"): the use of the V-form demonstrates a respectful attitude towards superiors and the elderly, while it can be sidestepped through the passive form.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

**Slovene** ([/ˈsloʊviːn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Slovene2.ogg "File:En-us-Slovene2.ogg") [*SLOH-veen*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") or [/sloʊˈviːn, slə-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*sloh-VEEN, slə-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")[[8]](#cite_note-8)) or **Slovenian** ([/sloʊˈviːniən, slə-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Slovenian.ogg "File:En-us-Slovenian.ogg") [*sloh-VEE-nee-ən, slə-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) *slovenščina* [[slɔˈʋèːnʃtʃina]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Slovene "Help:IPA/Slovene")) is a [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavic_languages "South Slavic languages") language of the [Balto-Slavic](/wiki/Balto-Slavic_languages "Balto-Slavic languages") branch of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"). Most of its 2.5 million speakers are the inhabitants of [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), the majority of them ethnic [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes"). As Slovenia is part of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), Slovene is also one of its 24 [official and working languages](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union"). Its grammar is highly [fusional](/wiki/Fusional_languages "Fusional languages"), and it has a [dual grammatical number](/wiki/Dual_(grammatical_number) "Dual (grammatical number)"), an archaic feature shared with some other Indo-European languages. Two accentual norms (one characterized by [pitch accent](/wiki/Pitch-accent_language "Pitch-accent language")) are used. Its flexible [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order") is often adjusted for emphasis or stylistic reasons, although basically it is an [SVO](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object_word_order "Subject–verb–object word order") language. It has a [T–V distinction](/wiki/T%E2%80%93V_distinction "T–V distinction"): the use of the V-form demonstrates a respectful attitude towards superiors and the elderly, while it can be sidestepped through the passive form.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Standard Slovene

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Standard Slovene")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Slovene_language "Special:EditPage/Slovene language") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(March 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Standard Slovene is the national [standard language](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") that was formed in the 18th and 19th centuries, based on the [Upper](/wiki/Upper_Carniolan_dialect_group "Upper Carniolan dialect group") and [Lower Carniolan dialect groups](/wiki/Lower_Carniolan_dialect_group "Lower Carniolan dialect group"), more specifically on the language of [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana") and adjacent areas. The Lower Carniolan dialect group was the dialect used in the 16th century by [Primož Trubar](/wiki/Primo%C5%BE_Trubar "Primož Trubar") for his writings, while he also used Slovene as spoken in Ljubljana,[[12]](#cite_note-12) since he lived in the city for more than 20 years. It was the speech of Ljubljana that Trubar took as a foundation of what later became standard Slovene, with small addition of his native speech, the Lower Carniolan dialect.[[13]](#cite_note-Osnove_Trubarjevega_jezika-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Trubar's choice was also later adopted by other [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") writers in the 16th century, and ultimately led to the formation of a more standard language. The Upper dialect was also used by most authors during the language revival in the 18th and early 19th centuries, and was also the language spoken by [France Prešeren](/wiki/France_Pre%C5%A1eren "France Prešeren"), who, like most Slovene writers and poets, lived and worked in Ljubljana, where the speech was growing closer to the Upper Carniolan dialect group.[[13]](#cite_note-Osnove_Trubarjevega_jezika-13) Unstandardized dialects are better preserved in regions of the [Slovene Lands](/wiki/Slovene_Lands "Slovene Lands") where compulsory schooling was in languages other than Standard Slovene, as was the case with the [Carinthian Slovenes](/wiki/Carinthian_Slovenes "Carinthian Slovenes") in [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), and the [Slovene minority in Italy](/wiki/Slovene_minority_in_Italy "Slovene minority in Italy"). For example, the [Resian](/wiki/Resian_dialect "Resian dialect") and [Torre](/wiki/Torre_Valley_dialect "Torre Valley dialect") (Ter) dialects in the Italian [Province of Udine](/wiki/Province_of_Udine "Province of Udine") differ most from other Slovene dialects.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classification")]

Slovene is an [Indo-European language](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") belonging to the Western subgroup of the [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavic_languages "South Slavic languages") branch of the [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"), together with [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian"). It is close to the [Chakavian](/wiki/Chakavian_dialect "Chakavian dialect") and especially [Kajkavian dialects](/wiki/Kajkavian_dialect "Kajkavian dialect") of Serbo-Croatian, but genealogically more distant from the [Shtokavian dialect](/wiki/Shtokavian_dialect "Shtokavian dialect"), the basis for the [Bosnian](/wiki/Bosnian_language "Bosnian language"), [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), [Montenegrin](/wiki/Montenegrin_language "Montenegrin language"), and [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language") standard languages. Slovene in general, and [Prekmurje Slovene](/wiki/Prekmurje_Slovene "Prekmurje Slovene") in particular, shares the highest level of [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with transitional Kajkavian dialects of [Hrvatsko Zagorje](/wiki/Hrvatsko_Zagorje "Hrvatsko Zagorje") and [Međimurje](/wiki/Me%C4%91imurje_County "Međimurje County").[[15]](#cite_note-Greenberg-15) Furthermore, Slovene shares certain linguistic characteristics with all South Slavic languages, including those of the Eastern subgroup, namely [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language"), [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonian_language "Macedonian language"), and [Torlakian dialects](/wiki/Torlakian_dialects "Torlakian dialects").

Mutual intelligibility with varieties of Serbo-Croatian is hindered by differences in vocabulary, grammar, and pronunciation,[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Kajkavian](/wiki/Kajkavian "Kajkavian") being firmly the most mutually intelligible. Slovene has some commonalities with the [West Slavic languages](/wiki/West_Slavic_language "West Slavic language") that are not found in other South Slavic languages.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Bri%C5%BEinski_spomeniki_3.png/250px-Bri%C5%BEinski_spomeniki_3.png)](/wiki/File:Bri%C5%BEinski_spomeniki_3.png)

The *[Freising manuscripts](/wiki/Freising_manuscripts "Freising manuscripts")*, dating from the late 10th or the early 11th century, are considered the oldest documents in Slovene.

Like all [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"), Slovene traces its roots to the same proto-Slavic group of languages that produced [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic"). The earliest known examples of a distinct, written dialect connected to Slovene are from the *[Freising manuscripts](/wiki/Freising_manuscripts "Freising manuscripts")*, known in Slovene as *Brižinski spomeniki*. The consensus estimate of their date of origin is between 972 and 1039 CE (most likely before 1000). These religious writings are among the oldest surviving manuscripts in any Slavic language.

The *Freising manuscripts* are a record of a proto-Slovene that was spoken in a more scattered territory than modern Slovene, which included most of the present-day Austrian states of [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(state) "Carinthia (state)") and [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"), as well as [East Tyrol](/wiki/East_Tyrol "East Tyrol"), the [Val Pusteria](/wiki/Val_Pusteria "Val Pusteria") in [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), and some areas of [Upper](/wiki/Upper_Austria "Upper Austria") and [Lower Austria](/wiki/Lower_Austria "Lower Austria").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Between the 9th and 12th century, proto-Slovene spread into northern [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria") and in the areas around [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste"). By the 15th century, most of the northern areas were gradually [Germanized](/wiki/Germanization "Germanization"): The northern border of the Slovene-speaking territory stabilized on the line going from north of [Klagenfurt](/wiki/Klagenfurt "Klagenfurt") to south of [Villach](/wiki/Villach "Villach") and east of [Hermagor](/wiki/Hermagor-Pressegger_See "Hermagor-Pressegger See") in Carinthia, while in Styria it was more or less identical with the current Austrian-Slovenian border. This linguistic border remained almost unchanged until the late 19th century, when a second process of Germanization took place, mostly in Carinthia.

During most of the Middle Ages, Slovene was a vernacular language of the peasantry, although it was also spoken in most of the towns on Slovenian territory, together with German or Italian. Although during this time German emerged as the spoken language of the nobility, Slovene had some role in the courtly life of the Carinthian, Carniolan, and Styrian nobility as well. This is proved by the survival of certain ritual formulas in Slovene (such as the ritual installation of the Dukes of Carinthia). The words *Buge waz primi, gralva Venus!* ('God be With You, Queen Venus!'), with which [Bernhard von Spanheim](/wiki/Bernhard_von_Spanheim "Bernhard von Spanheim") greeted the poet [Ulrich von Liechtenstein](/wiki/Ulrich_von_Liechtenstein "Ulrich von Liechtenstein"), who was travelling around Europe in guise of Venus, upon his arrival in Carinthia in 1227 (or 1238),[[20]](#cite_note-20) is another example of some level of Slovene knowledge among high nobility in the region.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The first printed Slovene words, *stara pravda* (meaning 'old justice' or 'old laws'), appeared in 1515 in [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") in a poem of the German mercenaries who suppressed the [Slovene peasant revolt](/wiki/Slovene_peasant_revolt_of_1515 "Slovene peasant revolt of 1515"): the term was presented as the peasants' motto and battle cry.[[22]](#cite_note-Cvirn2000-22) Standard Slovene emerged in the second half of the 16th century, thanks to the works of Slovene Lutheran authors, who were active during the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"). The most prominent authors from this period are [Primož Trubar](/wiki/Primo%C5%BE_Trubar "Primož Trubar"), who wrote the first books in Slovene; [Adam Bohorič](/wiki/Adam_Bohori%C4%8D "Adam Bohorič"), the author of the first Slovene grammar; and [Jurij Dalmatin](/wiki/Jurij_Dalmatin "Jurij Dalmatin"), who translated the entire [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") into Slovene.

From the high Middle Ages up to the dissolution of the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire") in 1918, in the territory of present-day Slovenia, [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") was the language of the elite, and Slovene was the language of the common people. During this period, German had a strong influence on Slovene; many [Germanisms](/wiki/Germanization "Germanization") are preserved in contemporary colloquial Slovene. Many Slovene [scientists](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist") before the 1920s also wrote in foreign languages, mostly German, which was the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* of science throughout [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") at the time.

### Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Recent history")]

During the rise of [Romantic nationalism](/wiki/Romantic_nationalism "Romantic nationalism") in the 19th century, the cultural movements of [Illyrism](/wiki/Illyrism "Illyrism") and [Pan-Slavism](/wiki/Pan-Slavism "Pan-Slavism") brought words from [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian"), specifically Croatian dialects, and [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language") into standard Slovene, mostly to replace words previously borrowed from [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"). Most of these innovations have remained, although some were dropped in later development. In the second half of the 19th century, many nationalist authors made an abundant use of Serbo-Croatian vocabulary but adapted it to Slovene orthography: among them were [Fran Levstik](/wiki/Fran_Levstik "Fran Levstik") and [Josip Jurčič](/wiki/Josip_Jur%C4%8Di%C4%8D "Josip Jurčič"), who wrote the first novel in Slovene in 1866. This tendency was reversed in the [Fin de siècle](/wiki/Fin_de_si%C3%A8cle "Fin de siècle") period by the first generation of modernist Slovene authors (most notably the writer [Ivan Cankar](/wiki/Ivan_Cankar "Ivan Cankar")), who resorted to a more purist and locally derived language without excessive Serbo-Croatian borrowings.

During the [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") in the 1920s and 1930s, the influence of Serbo-Croatian increased again. This was opposed by the younger generations of Slovene authors and intellectuals; among the fiercest opponents of an excessive Serbo-Croatian influence on Slovene were the intellectuals associated with the leftist journal *[Sodobnost](/wiki/Sodobnost "Sodobnost")*, as well as some younger [Catholic](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") activists and authors. After 1945, numerous Serbo-Croatian words that had been used in the previous decades were dropped. The result was that a Slovene text from the 1910s is frequently closer to modern Slovene than a text from the 1920s and 1930s.

Between 1920 and 1941, the official language of the Kingdom of Yugoslavia was defined as "Serbo-Croato-Slovene", which was in practice merely Serbo-Croatian. In Slovenia, however, Slovene remained in use in education and administration. Many state institutions used only Serbo-Croatian, and a Slovene–Serbo-Croatian bilingualism was applied in many spheres of public life in Slovenia. For example, at post offices, on railways, and in administrative offices, Serbo-Croatian was used alongside Slovene. However, state employees were expected to be able to speak Slovene in Slovenia.

During the same time, western Slovenia (the [Slovenian Littoral](/wiki/Slovenian_Littoral "Slovenian Littoral") and the western districts of [Inner Carniola](/wiki/Inner_Carniola "Inner Carniola")) was under Italian administration and subjected to a violent policy of [Fascist Italianization](/wiki/Fascist_Italianization "Fascist Italianization"); the same policy was applied to Slovene speakers in [Venetian Slovenia](/wiki/Venetian_Slovenia "Venetian Slovenia"), [Gorizia](/wiki/Gorizia "Gorizia"), and [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste"). Between 1923 and 1943, all public use of Slovene in these territories was strictly prohibited, and Slovene-language activists were persecuted by the state.

After the [Carinthian Plebiscite](/wiki/Carinthian_Plebiscite "Carinthian Plebiscite") of 1920, a less severe policy of [Germanization](/wiki/Germanization "Germanization") took place in the Slovene-speaking areas of southern [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(state) "Carinthia (state)") which remained under Austrian administration. After the [Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss") of 1938, the use of Slovene was strictly forbidden in Carinthia as well. This accelerated a process of [language shift](/wiki/Language_shift "Language shift") in Carinthia, which continued throughout the second half of the 20th century: according to the Austro-Hungarian census of 1910, around 21% of inhabitants of Carinthia spoke Slovene in their daily communication; by 1951, this figure had dropped to less than 10%, and by 2001 to a mere 2.8%.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Slovenia was divided among the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") of [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy#Fascist_regime_(1922–1943) "Kingdom of Italy"), [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), and [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"). Each of the occupying powers tried to either discourage or entirely suppress Slovene.

Following World War II, Slovenia became part of the [Federal Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"). While there was no official language at federal level, Serbo-Croatian dominated as prestige dialect in all aspects whereas Slovene remained confined to now [federal Slovenia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Slovenia "Socialist Republic of Slovenia") where it was made an official language recognized once again. In the territory of Slovenia, it was commonly used in almost all areas of public life. One important exception was the [Yugoslav army](/wiki/Yugoslav_People%27s_Army "Yugoslav People's Army"), where Serbo-Croatian was used exclusively, even in Slovenia.

National independence has further fortified the language: since 1991, when Slovenia gained independence, Slovene has been used as an official language in all areas of public life. In 2004, it became one of the official languages of the European Union upon the admission of Slovenia.

Nonetheless, the post-breakup influence of Serbo-Croatian on Slovene continued to a lesser extent, most prominently in [slang](/wiki/Slang "Slang") in [colloquial language](/wiki/Colloquial_language "Colloquial language").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[Joža Mahnič](/w/index.php?title=Jo%C5%BEa_Mahni%C4%8D&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joža Mahnič (page does not exist)"), a literary historian and president of the publishing house *[Slovenska matica](/wiki/Slovenska_matica "Slovenska matica")*, said in February 2008 that Slovene is a language rich enough to express everything, including the most sophisticated and specialised texts.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In February 2010, [Janez Dular](/w/index.php?title=Janez_Dular&action=edit&redlink=1 "Janez Dular (page does not exist)"), a prominent Slovene linguist, commented that, although Slovene is not an endangered language, its scope has been shrinking, especially in science and higher education.[[25]](#cite_note-Dular-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

The language is spoken by about 2.5 million people,[[27]](#cite_note-27) mainly in Slovenia, but also by [Slovene](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") ethnolinguistic minorities in [Friuli-Venezia Giulia](/wiki/Friuli-Venezia_Giulia "Friuli-Venezia Giulia"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") (around 60,000 in [Venetian Slovenia](/wiki/Venetian_Slovenia "Venetian Slovenia"), [Resia Valley](/wiki/Resia_Valley "Resia Valley"), [Canale Valley](/w/index.php?title=Canale_Valley&action=edit&redlink=1 "Canale Valley (page does not exist)"), [Province of Trieste](/wiki/Province_of_Trieste "Province of Trieste"), and in those municipalities of the [Province of Gorizia](/wiki/Province_of_Gorizia "Province of Gorizia") bordering Slovenia), in southern [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(state) "Carinthia (state)"), some parts of [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria") in [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria")[[28]](#cite_note-Fajon-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) (25,000), and in the western part of Croatian [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria") bordering Slovenia. It is also spoken in [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka") and [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb") (11,800-13,100), in southwestern [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") (3–4,000), in [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") (5,000), and by the Slovene diaspora throughout [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and the rest of the world (around 300,000), particularly in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (most notably [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), home to an estimated 3,400 speakers),[[32]](#cite_note-32) [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[15]](#cite_note-Greenberg-15)

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Dialects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Slovene_dialects.svg/250px-Slovene_dialects.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Slovene_dialects.svg)

A schematic map of Slovene dialects, based on the map by [Tine Logar](/wiki/Tine_Logar "Tine Logar"), [Jakob Rigler](/w/index.php?title=Jakob_Rigler&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jakob Rigler (page does not exist)"), and other sources

Main article: [Slovene dialects](/wiki/Slovene_dialects "Slovene dialects")

Slovene is sometimes characterized as the most diverse Slavic language in terms of [its dialects](/wiki/Slovene_dialects "Slovene dialects"),[[33]](#cite_note-MLD_2009-33) with different degrees of mutual intelligibility.[[34]](#cite_note-Vila2012-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Ruhlen1991-35) Accounts of the number of dialects range from as few as seven[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) dialects, often considered dialect groups or dialect bases that are further subdivided into as many as 50 dialects.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Other sources characterize the number of dialects as nine[[40]](#cite_note-40) or eight.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The Slovene proverb "Every village has its own voice" (*Vsaka vas ima svoj glas*) depicts the differences in dialects.

The [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje_Slovene "Prekmurje Slovene") dialect used to have a written norm of its own at one point.[[42]](#cite_note-Jan2000-42) The [Resian](/wiki/Resian_dialect "Resian dialect") dialects have an independent written norm that is used by their regional state institutions.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Speakers of those two dialects have considerable difficulties with being understood by speakers of other varieties of Slovene, needing to [code-switch](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") to Standard Slovene. Other dialects are mutually intelligible when speakers avoid the excessive usage of regionalisms.

Regionalisms are mostly limited to culinary and agricultural expressions, although there are many exceptions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Some [loanwords](/wiki/Loanwords "Loanwords") have become so deeply rooted in the local language that people have considerable difficulties in finding a standard expression for the dialect term (for instance, *kremšnita* meaning [a type of custard cake](/wiki/Cremeschnitte "Cremeschnitte") is *kremna rezina* in Standard Slovene, but the latter term is very rarely used in speech, being considered inappropriate for non-literary registers[*[where?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Naming_conventions_(geographic_names) "Wikipedia:Naming conventions (geographic names)")*]). Southwestern dialects incorporate many [calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque") and [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") from [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), whereas eastern and northwestern dialects are replete with lexemes of German origin. Usage of such words hinders intelligibility between dialects and is greatly discouraged in formal situations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Main article: [Slovene phonology](/wiki/Slovene_phonology "Slovene phonology")

Slovene has a [phoneme](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") set consisting of 21 [consonants](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") and 8 [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Consonants")]

All voiced [obstruents](/wiki/Obstruent "Obstruent") are devoiced at the end of words unless immediately followed by a word beginning with a vowel or a [voiced consonant](/wiki/Voiced_consonant "Voiced consonant"). In consonant clusters, the [voicing](/wiki/Voice_(phonetics) "Voice (phonetics)") distinction is neutralized and all consonants assimilate the voicing of the rightmost segment, i.e., the final consonant in the cluster. In this context, [v], [ɣ], and [d͡z] may occur as voiced allophones of /f/, /x/, and /t͡s/, respectively (e.g., *vŕh drevésa* [ʋrɣ dreˈʋesa]).[[46]](#cite_note-46)

/ʋ/ has several [allophones](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") depending on context.

* Before a vowel, pronunciation is labiodental, [[ʋ](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_approximant "Voiced labiodental approximant")][[47]](#cite_note-skp136-47) (also described as [[v](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_fricative "Voiced labiodental fricative")]).[[48]](#cite_note-gr18-48)
* After a vowel, pronunciation is bilabial [[w](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Voiced labial–velar approximant")] and forms a diphthong.[[47]](#cite_note-skp136-47)[[48]](#cite_note-gr18-48)
* At the beginning of a syllable, before a consonant (e.g., in *vsi* "all"), the pronunciation varies more widely by speaker and area. Many speakers convert /ʋ/ into a full vowel [u] in this position.[[47]](#cite_note-skp136-47)[[48]](#cite_note-gr18-48) For those speakers who retain a consonantal pronunciation, it is pronounced [[w](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Voiced labial–velar approximant")] before a voiced consonant and [[ʍ](/wiki/Voiceless_labial%E2%80%93velar_fricative "Voiceless labial–velar fricative")] before a voiceless consonant.[[47]](#cite_note-skp136-47)[[48]](#cite_note-gr18-48) Thus, *vsi* may be pronounced as disyllabic [uˈsi] or monosyllabic [ʍsi].

The sequences /lj/, /nj/, and /rj/ occur only before a vowel. Before a consonant or word-finally, they are reduced to /l/, /n/, and /r/, respectively. This is reflected in the spelling in the case of /rj/, but not for /lj/ and /nj/.

Under certain (somewhat unpredictable) circumstances, /l/ at the end of a syllable may become [w], merging with the allophone of /ʋ/ in that position.

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Slovene grammar](/wiki/Slovene_grammar "Slovene grammar")

### Nouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Nouns")]

Main article: [Slovene declension](/wiki/Slovene_declension "Slovene declension")

Slovene nouns retain six of the seven Slavic [noun cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case"): [nominative](/wiki/Nominative "Nominative"), [accusative](/wiki/Accusative "Accusative"), [genitive](/wiki/Genitive "Genitive"), [dative](/wiki/Dative "Dative"), [locative](/wiki/Locative "Locative"), and [instrumental](/wiki/Instrumental_case "Instrumental case"). There is no distinct [vocative](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case"); the nominative is used in that role. Nouns, adjectives, and pronouns have three numbers: singular, dual, and plural.

Nouns in Slovene are either masculine, feminine, or neuter gender. In addition, there is a distinction between animate and inanimate nouns. This is only relevant for masculine nouns and only in the singular, at odds with some other Slavic languages, e.g., [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), for which it is also relevant in the plural for all genders. Animate nouns have an accusative singular form that is identical to the genitive, while for inanimate nouns the accusative singular is the same as the nominative. Animacy is based mostly on semantics and is less rigid than gender. Generally speaking, a noun is animate if it refers to something that is generally thought to have free will or the ability to move of its own accord. This includes all nouns for people and animals. All other nouns are inanimate, including plants and other non-moving life forms, and also groups of people or animals. However, there are some nouns for inanimate objects that are generally animate, which mostly include inanimate objects that are named after people or animals. This includes:[[53]](#cite_note-53)

* Dead people or animals
* Makes of cars
* Certain diseases (named after animals)
* Certain devices (named after animals or people)
* Works of art (named after their creator)
* Chess pieces and playing cards (named for the people they represent)
* Wines and mushrooms (named as [demonyms](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym"))

### Definiteness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Definiteness")]

There are no [definite](/wiki/Definite_article "Definite article") or [indefinite articles](/wiki/Indefinite_article "Indefinite article") as in English (*the*, *a*, *an*) or [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") (*der, die, das, ein, eine*). A noun is described without articles; the [grammatical gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") is shown by the ending of the word. It is enough to say *barka* ('a' or 'the barge'), *Noetova barka* ('Noah's ark'). The gender is known in this case to be feminine. In [declensions](/wiki/Declension "Declension"), endings are normally changed; see below. If one would like to somehow distinguish between the definiteness or indefiniteness of a noun, one would say *(prav/natanko/ravno) tista barka* ('that/precise/exact barge') for 'the barge' and *neka/ena barka* ('some/a barge') for 'a barge'.

The definiteness of a noun phrase can also be discerned through the ending of the accompanying adjective. One should say *rdeči šotor* ('[exactly that] red tent') or *rdeč šotor* ('[a] red tent'). This difference is observable only for masculine nouns in nominative or accusative case. Because of the lack of article in Slovene and audibly insignificant difference between the masculine adjective forms, most dialects do not distinguish between definite and indefinite variants of the adjective, leading to [hypercorrection](/wiki/Hypercorrection "Hypercorrection") when speakers try to use Standard Slovene.[[54]](#cite_note-suss-54)

### T–V distinction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: T–V distinction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Jozef_Nahtigal_tombstone_in_Dobrova_Slovenia.jpg/250px-Jozef_Nahtigal_tombstone_in_Dobrova_Slovenia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jozef_Nahtigal_tombstone_in_Dobrova_Slovenia.jpg)

Tombstone of [Jožef Nahtigal](/w/index.php?title=Jo%C5%BEef_Nahtigal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jožef Nahtigal (page does not exist)") in [Dobrova](/wiki/Dobrova,_Dobrova%E2%80%93Polhov_Gradec "Dobrova, Dobrova–Polhov Gradec") with archaic Slovene *onikanje* in indirect reference. Literal translation "Here lie [*počivajo*] the honorable Jožef Nahtigal ... they were born [*rojeni*] ... they died [*umerli*] ... God grant them [*jim*] eternal peace and rest."

Slovene, like most other European languages, has a [T–V distinction](/wiki/T%E2%80%93V_distinction "T–V distinction"), or two forms of 'you' for formal and informal situations. Although informal address using the 2nd person singular *ti* form (known as *tikanje*) is officially limited to friends and family, talk among children, and addressing animals, it is increasingly used among the middle generation to signal a relaxed attitude or lifestyle instead of its polite or formal counterpart using the 2nd person plural *vi* form (known as *vikanje*).

An additional nonstandard but widespread use of a singular participle combined with a plural auxiliary verb (known as *polvikanje*) signals a somewhat more friendly and less formal attitude while maintaining politeness:

* *Vi ga niste videli.* ('You did not see him': both the auxiliary verb *niste* and the participle *videli* are plural masculine. Standard usage.)
* *Vi ga niste videl/videla.* ('You did not see him': the auxiliary verb *niste* is plural but the participle *videl/videla* is singular masculine/feminine. Nonstandard usage.)

The use of nonstandard forms (*polvikanje*) might be frowned upon by many people and would not likely be used in a formal setting.

The use of the 3rd person plural *oni* ('they') form (known as *onikanje* in both direct address and indirect reference; this is similar to using *Sie* in German) as an ultra-polite form is now archaic or dialectal. It is associated with servant-master relationships in older literature, the child-parent relationship in certain conservative rural communities, and parishioner-priest relationships.

### Foreign words

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Foreign words")]

Foreign words used in Slovene are of various types depending on the assimilation they have undergone. The types are:

* *sposojenka* (loanword) – fully assimilated; e.g., *pica* ('pizza').
* *tujka* (foreign word) – partly assimilated, either in writing and syntax or in pronunciation; e.g., *jazz*, *wiki*.
* *polcitatna beseda ali besedna zveza* (half-quoted word or phrase) – partly assimilated, either in writing and syntax or in pronunciation; e.g., *Shakespeare*, but *Shakespearja* in genitive case.
* *citatna beseda ali besedna zveza* (quoted word or phrase) – kept as in original, although pronunciation may be altered to fit into speech flow; e.g., *first lady* in all cases.

The loanwords are mostly from [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), while the more recently borrowed and less assimilated words are typically from [English](/wiki/English_language "English language").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Among the earliest borrowings in Slovene vocabulary are Romanisms, which began to enter the Slovene language with the settlement of Slovenia by the [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") and continued during the Middle Ages. These are primarily toponyms: Latin *Capris / Caprae* → [Koper](/wiki/Koper "Koper"), Latin *Sontius* → [Soča](/wiki/So%C4%8Da "Soča"). In addition, there are words such as *jambor* 'mast' < Latin *arbor* 'tree', *golida* 'milking pail' < [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") *galeda*, *hlače* 'trousers' < [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") *calcae*, *fant* 'boy, lad' < Italian *fante*.

Slovene vocabulary also contains a large number of [Germanisms](/wiki/Germanism_(linguistics) "Germanism (linguistics)"), borrowed from the 8th to the 19th centuries: *flinta* 'rifle' < German *Flinte*, *gmajna* 'community, common land' < [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German") *gemeine*, *krompir* 'potato' < German *Grundbirne*. Through German mediation, vocabulary of Latin and Greek origin also entered Slovene: *klošter* 'monastery', *škrinja* 'chest', *špital* 'hospital'.

## Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Writing system")]

Main articles: [Slovene alphabet](/wiki/Slovene_alphabet "Slovene alphabet") and [Slovene braille](/wiki/Slovene_braille "Slovene braille")

See also: [Bohorič alphabet](/wiki/Bohori%C4%8D_alphabet "Bohorič alphabet"), [Metelko alphabet](/wiki/Metelko_alphabet "Metelko alphabet"), and [Dajnko alphabet](/wiki/Dajnko_alphabet "Dajnko alphabet")

This alphabet (*abeceda*) was derived in the mid-1840s from [the system](/wiki/Gaj%27s_Latin_Alphabet "Gaj's Latin Alphabet") created by the [Croatian](/wiki/Croats "Croats") linguist [Ljudevit Gaj](/wiki/Ljudevit_Gaj "Ljudevit Gaj"). Intended for the [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian") language (in all its varieties), it was patterned on the [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language") alphabet of the 1830s. Before that /s/ was, for example, written as ⟨[ʃ](/wiki/%C6%A9 "Ʃ")⟩, ⟨ʃʃ⟩, or [⟨ſ⟩](/wiki/Long_s "Long s"); /tʃ/ as ⟨tʃch⟩, ⟨cz⟩, ⟨tʃcz⟩, or ⟨tcz⟩; /i/ sometimes as ⟨y⟩; /j/ as ⟨y⟩; /l/ as ⟨ll⟩; /ʋ/ as ⟨w⟩; /ʒ/ as ⟨ʃ⟩, ⟨ʃʃ⟩, or ⟨ʃz⟩.

The standard Slovene orthography, employed in almost all situations, uses only the letters of the [ISO basic Latin alphabet](/wiki/ISO_basic_Latin_alphabet "ISO basic Latin alphabet") plus ⟨č⟩, ⟨š⟩, and ⟨ž⟩. The letters ⟨q⟩, ⟨w⟩, ⟨x⟩, and ⟨y⟩ are not included:

The orthography thus underdifferentiates several phonemic distinctions:

* Stress, vowel length and tone are not distinguished, except with optional diacritics when it is necessary to distinguish between similar words with a different meaning.
* The two distinct mid-vowels are also not distinguished, both written as simply ⟨e⟩ and ⟨o⟩.
* The schwa /ə/ is also written as ⟨e⟩. However, the combination /ər/ is written as simply ⟨r⟩ between consonants and is thus distinguishable.
* Vocalized *l* /uʷ/ is written also as ⟨l⟩, but cannot be predictably distinguished from /l/ in that position.

In the tonemic varieties of Slovene, the ambiguity is even greater: ⟨e⟩ in a final syllable can stand for any of /éː/ /èː/ /ɛ́ː/ /ɛ̀ː/ /ɛ/ /ə/ (although /ɛ̀ː/ is rare; and Slovene, except in some dialects, does not distinguish tonemic accentuation).

The reader is expected to gather the interpretation of the word from the context, as in these examples:

* *gol*:
  + /ˈɡɔ́w/ gȍł "naked"
  + /ˈɡóːl/ gọ̑l "goal"
* *jesen*:
  + /ˈjɛ̀ːsɛn/ jésen "ash tree"
  + /jɛˈséːn/ jesẹ̑n "autumn"
* *kot*
  + /ˈkòːt/ kọ́t "angle"
  + /kɔt/ kot "as"
* *med*
  + /mɛt/ med "between"
  + /ˈméːt/ mẹ̑d "honey"
* *pol*
  + /ˈpóːl/ pọ̑l "pole"
  + /ˈpóːw/ pọ̑ł "half"
* *precej*
  + /ˈprɛ́tsɛj/ prȅcej "at once" (archaic)
  + /prɛˈtséːj/ precẹ̑j or /prɛˈtsɛ́j/ precȅj "a great deal (of)"

### Diacritics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Diacritics")]

To compensate for the shortcomings of the standard orthography, Slovene also uses standardized [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") or accent marks to denote [stress](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)"), [vowel length](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length"), and [pitch accent](/wiki/Pitch-accent_language "Pitch-accent language"), much like the closely related [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian"). However, as in Serbo-Croatian, use of such accent marks is restricted to dictionaries, language textbooks, and linguistic publications. In normal writing, the diacritics are almost never used, except in a few minimal pairs where real ambiguity could arise.

Two different and mutually-incompatible systems of diacritics are used. The first is the simpler non-tonemic system, which can be applied to all Slovene dialects. It is more widely used and is the standard representation in dictionaries such as SSKJ. The tonemic system also includes tone as part of the representation. However, neither system reliably distinguishes schwa /ə/ from the front mid-vowels, nor vocalised l /w/ from regular l /l/. Some sources, such as [Maks Pleteršnik](/wiki/Maks_Pleter%C5%A1nik "Maks Pleteršnik")'s 1894/95 dictionary, write these as *ə* and *ł*, respectively, but this is not as common.

#### Non-tonemic diacritics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Non-tonemic diacritics")]

In the non-tonemic system, the distinction between the two mid-vowels is indicated, as well as the placement of stress and the length of vowels:

* Long stressed vowels are notated with an acute diacritic: ⟨á é í ó ú ŕ⟩ (IPA: /aː eː iː oː uː ər/).
* However, the rarer long stressed low-mid vowels /ɛː/ and /ɔː/ are notated with a circumflex: ⟨ê ô⟩.
* Short stressed vowels are notated with a grave: ⟨à è ì ò ù⟩ (IPA: /a ɛ i ɔ u/). Some systems may also include ⟨ə̀⟩ for /ə/.

#### Tonemic diacritics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Tonemic diacritics")]

The tonemic system uses the diacritics somewhat differently from the non-tonemic system. The high-mid vowels /eː/ and /oː/ are written ⟨ẹ ọ⟩ with a subscript dot, while the low-mid vowels /ɛː/ and /ɔː/ are written as plain ⟨e o⟩.

Pitch accent and vowel length is indicated by four diacritical marks:

* The [acute](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent") ( ´ ) indicates **long and low** pitch: ⟨á é ẹ́ í ó ọ́ ú ŕ⟩ (IPA: /àː ɛ̀ː èː ìː ɔ̀ː òː ùː ə̀r/).
* The [inverted breve](/wiki/Inverted_breve "Inverted breve") (  ̑ ) indicates **long and high** pitch: ⟨ȃ ȇ ẹ̑ ȋ ȏ ọ̑ ȗ ȓ⟩ (IPA: /áː ɛ́ː éː íː ɔ́ː óː úː ə́r/).
* The [grave](/wiki/Grave_accent "Grave accent") ( ` ) indicates **short and low** pitch. This occurs only on ⟨è⟩ (IPA: /ə̀/), optionally written as ⟨ə̀⟩.
* The [double grave](/wiki/Double_grave "Double grave") (  ̏ ) indicates **short and high** pitch: ⟨ȁ ȅ ȉ ȍ ȕ⟩ (IPA: á ɛ́ í ɔ́ ú). ⟨ȅ⟩ is also used for /ə́/, optionally written as ⟨ə̏⟩.

The schwa vowel /ə/ is written ambiguously as ⟨e⟩, but its accentuation will sometimes distinguish it: a long vowel mark can never appear on a schwa, while a grave accent can appear only on a schwa. Thus, only ⟨ȅ⟩ and unstressed ⟨e⟩ are truly ambiguous.

## Regulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Regulation")]

Standard Slovene spelling and grammar are defined by the Orthographic Committee and the Fran Ramovš Institute of the Slovene Language, which are both part of the [Slovenian Academy of Sciences and Arts](/wiki/Slovenian_Academy_of_Sciences_and_Arts "Slovenian Academy of Sciences and Arts") (*Slovenska akademija znanosti in umetnosti*, SAZU). The newest reference book of standard Slovene spelling (and to some extent also grammar) is the *Slovenski pravopis* (*SP2001*; Slovene Normative Guide). The latest printed edition was published in 2001 (reprinted in 2003 with some corrections) and contains more than 130,000 dictionary entries. In 2003, an electronic version was published.

The official dictionary of modern Slovene, which was also prepared by SAZU, is *Slovar slovenskega knjižnega jezika* (*SSKJ*; Standard Slovene Dictionary). It was published in five volumes by Državna Založba Slovenije between 1970 and 1991 and contains more than 100,000 entries and subentries with accentuation, part-of-speech labels, common collocations, and various qualifiers. In the 1990s, an electronic version of the dictionary was published and is available online.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-Portal-56)

The SAZU considers SP2001 to be the normative source on Slovene. When dictionary entries in SP2001 and SSKJ differ, the SP2001 entry takes precedence. SP2001 is called a Spelling Dictionary by the European Network of e-Lexicography.[[56]](#cite_note-Portal-56)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sample text")]

Article 1 of the *[Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights")* in Slovene and English.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

:   **Slovene:** *Vsi ljudje se rodijo svobodni ter imajo enako dostojanstvo in pravice. Dana sta jim razum in vest, in bi morali drug z drugim ravnati v duhu bratstva.*

:   **English:** *All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.*
